schools.summary: Gill: 'the elder' is the writer of the epistle, the Apostle John, who so styles himself on account of his age and office, as in the preceding epistle; the person to whom he writes is 'the wellbeloved Gaius' (not necessarily the Gaius who was the Apostle Paul's host, though their characters agree, both being hospitable); John writing as the elder to the wellbeloved Gaius, whom he loves in the truth — the affectionate salutation to a beloved fellow-believer (John, opening this short personal letter, naming himself 'the elder,' a title of age and office, the same he used in his second epistle, and addressing 'the wellbeloved Gaius,' a beloved Christian friend distinguished by the apostle's warm regard, 'whom I love in the truth'; the ground and mark of this love, that John loves Gaius 'in the truth,' a love founded on and bounded by the gospel truth, a sincere and spiritual affection not a mere natural fondness, the kind of love that binds true believers in Christ, so the letter opens with the warm, truth-grounded love of the aged apostle for a faithful friend).

themes: the elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius
whom I love in the truth
the affectionate salutation to a beloved friend
a love founded on the gospel truth

schools.summary: Gill: John wishes that Gaius may prosper and succeed in all temporal affairs, in the business of life; and as success of this sort depends upon the blessing of God, which maketh rich, it is to be wished and prayed for from him; Barnes: the word rendered 'I wish' means rather 'I pray'; John praying that Gaius may prosper and be in health, even as his soul prospers — the prayer for the friend's outward welfare measured by his thriving soul (John, with tender affection, making a prayerful wish for Gaius's whole welfare: 'beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health,' that he may succeed in his temporal affairs and enjoy bodily health, 'even as thy soul prospereth'; the high implied commendation, that John wishes Gaius's outward state to match his inward, 'even as thy soul prospereth,' which takes for granted that Gaius's soul was in a flourishing, thriving condition, so the apostle prays his friend may be as healthy and prosperous in body and estate as he already was in his soul, a wish that would be a poor thing for many whose souls fare worse than their bodies, but a rich blessing for the spiritually thriving Gaius).

themes: beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health
even as thy soul prospereth
the prayer for welfare measured by the thriving soul
wishing the outward to match the flourishing inward

schools.summary: Gill: the brethren came from the place where Gaius lived to where John now resided; these brethren seem to be preachers of the word who travelled from place to place to spread the gospel; Matthew Henry: the good report the apostle had received concerning this friend of his; John declaring that he rejoiced greatly when the brethren came and testified of the truth in Gaius, even as he walks in the truth — the apostle's great joy at the reported truth and consistent walk of Gaius (John giving the ground of his confidence in Gaius's flourishing soul: 'for I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee,' the travelling brethren bringing a good report, witnessing to the genuine truth and grace dwelling in Gaius, 'even as thou walkest in the truth'; the twofold testimony, not only 'the truth that is in thee,' the truth received and held in the heart, but 'as thou walkest in the truth,' the truth lived out and shown in his conduct, the inward truth proved by the outward walk, so what gave John such great joy was the report that Gaius both held the truth and walked in it).

themes: for I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee
even as thou walkest in the truth
John's joy at the reported truth and walk of Gaius
the inward truth proved by the outward walk

schools.summary: Barnes: there is no greater joy than to hear that one's children adhere steadfastly to the truth and that they live in accordance with it; Gill: nothing causes greater joy than this (the Vulgate reading 'grace' or 'thanks,' that he had nothing to be more thankful for); John declaring that he has no greater joy than to hear that his children walk in truth — the highest joy of the spiritual father, to see his children walking in the truth (John telling the spring of his great rejoicing: 'I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth'; the depth of this feeling, that John, the aged apostle and spiritual father of many converts, 'my children,' those begotten through his ministry, counts no joy greater than to hear that they 'walk in truth,' hold the truth and live by it, so the faithful walk of his spiritual children was the apostle's deepest delight, dearer to him than any earthly joy, and herein he sets the pattern for every faithful minister and parent in the gospel, whose highest happiness is the godliness of those in their care).

themes: I have no greater joy
than to hear that my children walk in truth
the highest joy of the spiritual father
the faithful walk of his children his deepest delight

schools.summary: Gill: Gaius did a faithful thing, and as became a faithful man, a believer in Christ; in all his beneficence and charity he acted the upright part, not in a hypocritical way to be seen of men and gain applause, but from a principle of love and with a view to the glory of God; John commending Gaius that he does faithfully whatsoever he does to the brethren and to strangers — the faithful, sincere hospitality of Gaius to known and unknown believers (John turning to praise Gaius's particular grace, his hospitality: 'beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers'; two things commended, first the manner, 'faithfully,' Gaius doing his good works sincerely from a true heart and a right principle, not for show but as a faithful believer acting uprightly before God, and second the breadth, 'to the brethren, and to strangers,' his kindness reaching not only the believers he knew but also the 'strangers,' the travelling brethren he had never met before, so Gaius's hospitality was both genuine in its spring and wide in its reach, embracing known and unknown alike for Christ's sake).

themes: beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren
and to strangers
the faithful, sincere hospitality of Gaius
genuine in spring and wide in reach

schools.summary: Gill: the brethren, coming to the church at Ephesus where John was, and being greatly affected with Gaius's kindness and liberality to them, could not forbear speaking of it to his praise in the presence of the members of the church, as well as acquainting the Apostle John with it; John saying that the brethren bore witness of Gaius's charity before the church, and that if he brings them forward on their journey in a godly manner, he does well — the public report of Gaius's love and the charge to forward the travelling brethren (John telling how Gaius's kindness was made known and what further he might do: 'which have borne witness of thy charity before the church,' the brethren so helped publicly testifying to Gaius's love and liberality before the assembled church, 'whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well'; the two parts, the report, his charity openly praised before the church, and the further duty, to 'bring [them] forward on their journey,' to furnish and forward the travelling preachers on their way, supplying their needs, 'after a godly sort,' in a manner befitting God and the gospel, so John commends what Gaius had done and gently urges him to continue forwarding the Lord's servants worthily).

themes: which have borne witness of thy charity before the church
whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well
the report of Gaius's charity and the charge to forward the brethren
forwarding the preachers in a manner befitting God

schools.summary: Gill: that for his name's sake they went forth from Judea, either of their own accord to preach the gospel, or being driven out by the unbelieving Jews for professing the name of Christ; be it which it will, there was good reason why they should be regarded, especially since they took nothing of the Gentiles; John giving the reason that for Christ's name's sake the brethren went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles — the self-denying preachers worthy of support because they served Christ and took nothing from the heathen (John giving the ground of the duty to forward these brethren: 'because that for his name's sake they went forth,' they having gone out in service of Christ's name and gospel to preach it abroad, 'taking nothing of the Gentiles'; the double commendation, first their motive and errand, 'for his name's sake,' they labouring and travelling purely for Christ's honour and the spread of his gospel not for gain, and second their self-denial, 'taking nothing of the Gentiles,' refusing to take support from the unconverted heathen that the gospel might be free of charge and above the suspicion of covetousness, so that, since they would take nothing from the world they served, the believers ought the more readily to support them).

themes: because that for his name's sake they went forth
taking nothing of the Gentiles
the self-denying preachers worthy of support
the gospel free of charge, above suspicion of gain

schools.summary: Gill: 'we ought to receive such' preachers of the word into our houses, and entertain them cheerfully while they continue, and supply them with all necessaries when they depart; John declaring that we ought to receive such, that we might be fellow-helpers to the truth — the duty of hospitality to the Lord's servants, making the host a partner in the gospel (John drawing the conclusion from the brethren's self-denial: 'we therefore ought to receive such,' it being our duty to welcome and support such preachers, receiving them into our homes and supplying their needs, 'that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth'; the honour of this service, that those who receive and help the travelling preachers become 'fellowhelpers to the truth,' partners and co-workers in the spread of the gospel, who, though they do not themselves go out to preach, by supporting those who do share in the work and its reward, so hospitality to the Lord's servants is no small thing but a real fellowship in the cause of the truth).

themes: we therefore ought to receive such
that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth
the duty of hospitality making the host a partner in the gospel
sharing the work and reward by supporting the preachers

schools.summary: Gill: John wrote to the church where Gaius was a member (some take this of the church at Corinth, if Gaius be Paul's host, but it seems rather some church in Asia nearer Ephesus); but Diotrephes, who loved preeminence, received not the apostle nor his letter; John saying that he wrote to the church, but Diotrephes, who loves to have the preeminence among them, receives them not — the ambitious church-leader who rejected the apostle's authority (John turning to a sad contrast with the faithful Gaius: 'I wrote unto the church,' John having sent a letter to the church, 'but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not'; the character laid bare, that Diotrephes was a man in the church, perhaps a leader, marked by ambition, who 'loveth to have the preeminence,' craved the first place, to rule and be chief above all, and from this proud root sprang his sin: he 'receiveth us not,' refusing to acknowledge or submit to John and the brethren the apostle commended, rejecting even apostolic authority, so the love of preeminence is named as the spring of his unruly and divisive conduct).

themes: I wrote unto the church
but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not
the ambitious church-leader rejecting the apostle
the love of preeminence the spring of his divisive conduct

schools.summary: Gill: if John comes, where both Gaius and Diotrephes lived, as he trusted he should shortly, he will remember his deeds; Barnes: he would punish his arrogance and presumption, would take measures to deal with it; John resolving that, if he comes, he will remember Diotrephes' deeds, his malicious prating, his refusing the brethren, forbidding others, and casting them out — the fourfold wickedness of Diotrephes and the apostle's purpose to confront it (John laying out the full account of Diotrephes' wrongdoing and his resolve to deal with it: 'wherefore, if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth'; the rising scale of his sin, first 'prating against us with malicious words,' idle slanderous talk against the apostle and the brethren, and 'not content therewith,' he went further, he 'neither doth he himself receive the brethren,' refusing hospitality to the travelling preachers, 'and forbiddeth them that would,' hindering others who wished to receive them, 'and casteth them out of the church,' excommunicating those who showed such hospitality, so his ambition issued in slander, inhospitality, the forbidding of others, and tyrannical expulsion, a sad picture of unchecked pride in the church which the apostle would openly confront).

themes: if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth
prating against us with malicious words
neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out
the fourfold wickedness of Diotrephes confronted

schools.summary: Gill: 'follow not that which is evil' in general, it being hateful to God, contrary to his nature and will, and bad in itself as well as pernicious in its consequences; and particularly follow not or imitate not the evils in Diotrephes, his pride, ambition, love of preeminence, and tyrannical government; John charging the beloved to follow not evil but good, since he that does good is of God but he that does evil has not seen God — the call to imitate the good, not Diotrephes' evil, with the mark of the true child of God (John, drawing the lesson from the bad example, exhorting Gaius and all: 'beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good,' do not imitate the evil such as Diotrephes' pride and tyranny but copy the good such as Gaius's hospitality, and giving the searching test: 'he that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God'; the dividing rule, that the habitual doer of good shows himself to be 'of God,' born of and belonging to God, while the doer of evil 'hath not seen God,' has no true knowledge or experience of God whatever he professes, so conduct reveals the heart, and the one who does evil, like Diotrephes, gives proof he does not truly know God).

themes: beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good
he that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God
the call to follow good, not Diotrephes' evil
conduct revealing the heart

schools.summary: Gill: Demetrius was of a quite different cast from Diotrephes, and therefore the apostle makes mention of him to Gaius, to be followed by him and not the other; he was either the same with Demas (which is a contraction of this name) or the person John sent from Ephesus with this letter; John commending Demetrius, who has good report of all men, and of the truth itself, and of the apostle, whose witness is true — the threefold witness to the good Demetrius, set against Diotrephes (John holding up a third figure, a good example to set beside the bad: 'Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself: yea, and we also bear record'; the threefold testimony to his character, first 'of all men,' a good report from all who knew him, even the world acknowledging his worth, second 'of the truth itself,' his life agreeing with the truth so that the gospel itself as it were bore witness to him, his conduct matching its standard, and third 'we also bear record,' the apostle's own testimony added and confirmed, 'ye know that our record is true,' so Demetrius, unlike Diotrephes, was a man of approved and well-attested godliness, worthy to be followed).

themes: Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself
yea, and we also bear record; and ye know that our record is true
the threefold witness to the good Demetrius
a good example set against Diotrephes

schools.summary: Gill: John had many things to write, with regard to churches, and particular persons, and concerning hospitality to the poor brethren; Barnes: this epistle closes, as the second does, with a statement that he had many things to say but that he preferred waiting till he should see him rather than put them on paper; John saying that he had many things to write, but would not write them with ink and pen — the apostle's many things deferred to a personal meeting (John, drawing to a close as in his second epistle, saying he has more to communicate but chooses not to commit it all to writing: 'I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee'; the reason, which the next verse gives, that John had much on his heart concerning churches, persons, and the care of the poor brethren, but rather than set it all down 'with ink and pen' he preferred to reserve it for a personal meeting, where heart could speak to heart more freely and fully than letters allow, so the brevity of the letter is not from lack of matter but from the hope of a near and fuller conversation face to face).

themes: I had many things to write
but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee
the many things deferred to a personal meeting
reserving it for heart-to-heart conversation

schools.summary: Gill: John trusts he shall shortly see Gaius, either at Ephesus where John was or rather at the place where Gaius lived; Barnes: 'our friends salute thee,' that is, your friends and mine, referring rather to their mutual private friends than to Christians generally; John trusting that he will shortly see Gaius and speak face to face, with the benediction of peace and the exchange of friendly greetings — the hope of a face-to-face meeting and the warm, personal close (John ending the letter with the same warmth that fills it: 'but I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face,' the hoped-for personal visit when they would talk together freely, 'Peace be to thee,' the benediction of peace upon his friend, 'Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name'; the tender, personal note of the close, that the mutual friends of John and Gaius send their greetings, and John bids Gaius 'greet the friends by name,' each one individually, a touch of warm particular affection, so this little letter closes, as it began, in the warm fellowship and personal love of the Christian family).

themes: but I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face
Peace be to thee. Our friends salute thee
Greet the friends by name
the hope of a face-to-face meeting and the warm, personal close
